items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi. Honestly, I’m just feeling squeezed month to month, and I don’t want to mess this up as a single parent.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical and not judge-y. Can I start with income—what’s your gross income for the year, all-in?
Client: All-in gross is 77750 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly salary, or do you have bonus or variable comp mixed in?
Client: Both. Salary is 31750 annually, and then bonus is 46000 annually—my bonus is kind of the swingy part.

Client: Both. Salary is 31750 annually, and then bonus is 46000 annually—my bonus is kind of the swingy part.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Switching to spending—when you look at everything going out each month, what’s your total monthly expenses number right now?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 6150 a month. That’s like everything—food, childcare, utilities, all of it.
Advisor: Thanks. And within that, the debt payments are the part that tends to pinch. What are you paying monthly toward debts in total?
Client: Monthly debt payments total 4450.

Client: Total monthly expenses are 6150 a month. That’s like everything—food, childcare, utilities, all of it.
Advisor: Thanks. And within that, the debt payments are the part that tends to pinch. What are you paying monthly toward debts in total?
Client: Monthly debt payments total 4450.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s break that down just so I’m not missing anything—mortgage first. What’s the monthly payment on the mortgage?
Client: The mortgage payment is 3300 per month.

Client: Balance outstanding is 44400, and the interest rate is 9.81%.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. That lines up with the 4450 total you gave me. Now, on the asset side, I want to understand what you have available versus what’s locked up. What do your investable assets total, roughly?
Client: Investable assets total 158700.
Advisor: And what makes that up—cash, brokerage, retirement—can you walk me through the buckets?
Client: Sure. I have 22300 at the bank in cash.

Advisor: And what makes that up—cash, brokerage, retirement—can you walk me through the buckets?
Client: Sure. I have 22300 at the bank in cash.
Client: Then 84300 in a taxable brokerage account.
Client: Retirement is 32600 in my 401k/IRA at a brokerage.
Client: And I’ve got 19450 in private markets on a retirement platform.

Advisor: Okay, Jennifer—thanks. I want to zoom in on the debt totals for a second, just so we’re not missing anything that’s hiding in the weeds.
Advisor: If you add up everything you owe—mortgage plus the card—what do you have as total loans outstanding right now?
Client: Total loans outstanding is 106550.
Advisor: Got it. And just to sanity-check the split: what’s the mortgage balance outstanding today?
Client: Mortgage balance outstanding is 62200.

Client: The mortgage payment is 3300 per month.
Advisor: And the mortgage balance and rate—what are those today?
Client: Balance outstanding is 62200, and the interest rate is 6.9%.
Advisor: Thanks. Do you know when that mortgage is scheduled to be fully paid off—your final payment date?
Client: Yes, final payment date is 2031-10-28.

Client: Credit card payment is 1150 per month.
Advisor: And the card balance and interest rate?
Client: Balance outstanding is 44400, and the interest rate is 9.81%.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. That lines up with the 4450 total you gave me. Now, on the asset side, I want to understand what you have available versus what’s locked up. What do your investable assets total, roughly?
Client: Investable assets total 158700.

Advisor: Okay. And for planning purposes, what state are you a resident of right now?
Client: It’s listed as Other.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Now, switching gears a bit: when you think about investing, how would you describe your risk tolerance—conservative, moderate, aggressive?
Client: Aggressive. I’m comfortable taking risk, even if it’s bumpy.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m hearing you right—aggressive on the investing side, but the monthly payments on the mortgage and card are what make it feel tight day-to-day?

Client: All-in gross is 77750 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly salary, or do you have bonus or variable comp mixed in?
Client: Both. Salary is 31750 annually, and then bonus is 46000 annually—my bonus is kind of the swingy part.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Switching to spending—when you look at everything going out each month, what’s your total monthly expenses number right now?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 6150 a month. That’s like everything—food, childcare, utilities, all of it.

Advisor: Thanks. Do you know when that mortgage is scheduled to be fully paid off—your final payment date?
Client: Yes, final payment date is 2031-10-28.
Advisor: Perfect. Then the other big one you mentioned is the credit card—what’s the monthly payment you’re making there?
Client: Credit card payment is 1150 per month.
Advisor: And the card balance and interest rate?

Client: All-in gross is 77750 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And is that mostly salary, or do you have bonus or variable comp mixed in?
Client: Both. Salary is 31750 annually, and then bonus is 46000 annually—my bonus is kind of the swingy part.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. Switching to spending—when you look at everything going out each month, what’s your total monthly expenses number right now?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 6150 a month. That’s like everything—food, childcare, utilities, all of it.

Client: I’m single.
Advisor: Thanks. Now, taxes—do you know what bracket you usually land in? Even a ballpark is fine.
Client: I think it’s around the mid-20s? Like, 24%.
Advisor: Okay, 24%—that helps. And just so I’m not making assumptions: that’s your federal bracket you’re thinking of, right?
Client: Yes—24% federal.

Advisor: Hi Jennifer—good to meet you. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what made you reach out now?
Client: Hi. Honestly, I’m just feeling squeezed month to month, and I don’t want to mess this up as a single parent.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical and not judge-y. Can I start with income—what’s your gross income for the year, all-in?
Client: All-in gross is 77750 a year.

Client: It’s on an advisor platform.
Advisor: Perfect. And what value do you want me to use for the home today—just your best current estimate is fine.
